Every time you had a new client, had to duplicate this five, six different workflows, and each workflow has 30 different nodes with different prompts on each, and then it's going to apply one change to a prompt, to everything.
You spend a week just clicking around, and that was one problem.

And another problem was that the two that we're using, the main tool we're using, AirOps, could only run 12 things at once, and we had already like 20 clients, like 15 clients-ish, 15 to 20, and 12 things at once, like sometimes we'd be running like a thousand things, so it would take, or like 3,000 things for one client, and it would take like the whole night, and each row would be like, they would also charge us $1, or like 50 cents.
So it just became like management nightmare, from the coding management perspective, and then a bottleneck, and a huge cost that they're charging, instead of $30,000.
So we ended up moving everything out of this platform and created an AI framework similar to LangChain, but in TypeScript and designed for code generation.
So we reorganized the folders in the way that you have the prompts, you have the steps of the workflows, and you have the workflow definition, have the documentation all together.
So you can have cursor or client generate the workflows for you, then iterate on them, and then you deploy and you see the visual execution of everything.
And the next step for us will be to build the environment where somebody that's not technical like Marcel can do it themselves.
So it would be essentially very similar to like Lovable or VZero or both, but for the kind of work we do.

And the way I'm thinking about this and what we teach our investors is essentially that AI enabled service as software instead of software as a service.
It's like kind of flipped the game in a way where you can have, you can have one person.

Operating a set of tools and that person can perform the job of many other people before and it's mainly because of three things like now we have essentially like a basic reasoning engine so like it's really good something that's really good at making basic decisions in high volume for example we have a workflow for reviewing resumes and it like our workflows perform better than our recruiters because you just can't review a thousand people properly with a proper rubric so that's what I mean by a basic reasoning engine so you get better results than recruiters if you have the rubric set up properly and if you calibrate it enough and then another thing is let just move you to the side so I can see you and then another thing that is enabled is that things can are reproducible at scale if you can break it down to proper workflows so like same way you guys are thinking about the workflows in Cleary it takes a lot the way
For a person to be like, okay, this is the job, and this is the first task, this is the second task, this is the third task, this is the fourth task.
And then once you do that, and you actually have the smaller requests for the LLM, and you break down the smaller tasks, it does pretty well.
And it does at scale.
And the third thing is that you always need supervision, and it's less and less.
But you're always going to need humans to have the taste, or to calibrate, or to come up with the strategy.
But then you need an environment where you can do all these two things.
You understand that's a reasoning engine.
You understand that it can reproduce things at scale if this pack is clear.

Then what you really actually need to put things into practice is how do you define the work that must be done?

Workflows for workflow creation or like LLM-based frameworks, you're essentially creating prompts and you're stitching them together and you're tying with tools and some other things.
But at a higher level thinking, these things are like a spec for the work that you want it to be done.

So it's almost like in Terraform, for example, in Marcel's Hashtag Core background, you're defining the spec of the infrastructure and you have essentially an EMO file with the spec of everything you have on your servers.
And for working with AI, defining the workflow is part of defining the work that must be done.
And so that's one of the main reasons why we went with our own framework, because we want to control that, because we want to build on top of it.
And I can show you more.
So that's one of things, how do you define the work and then how do you perform the work?
So that's like a big part that I think things like Langche, take it from...
They just assume that the execution is the easy part, but then you have the entire company.

I know this is from my background at IFTTT, the runtime team was like 16 of the best engineers I've ever worked with, and that's what it took to run a distributed system with hundreds of APIs failing at any time and having to rewind back or servers failing and where do you restart your KafkaQ and stitching all these things together and making the protocols to be able to do many different kinds of APIs and roll out new versions and all that, and being able to back off of different scheduling.
So if you're dealing with lights, you back off a certain way, so you can ignore the data and just dump it.
But if you're dealing with somebody that's sending a bunch of things to Salesforce, then they actually have to retry until it works.
So figuring out all those details is not an easy task.
So, and things like link chain, they just...
Don't take that too much in consideration.
And then they roll out their platform after and things like that.
The ways to perform the work consistently for us took shape of like running everything on top of Temporo.
So Temporo is a distributed, like building an abstraction layer on top of Temporo instead of trying to rewrite the time from scratch.
And Temporo is a microservice architecture orchestration that came out of like some of the, I think it came out of AWS if I remember correctly.
But a lot of the big players use it, so it's very reliable.
It's not something that's in a shaky foundation like link chain or some of the newer players in the AI space.
But this just happens to be great for AI because it's, AIs are LLMs that have, LLMs are essentially APIs that have unreliable results and inconsistent and they fail all the time.
So that's, and Temporo was designed for dealing with API or
Illustrations for microservices and companies like Uber and Stripe and Netflix, and they have an army of microservices that can fail at any time.
So it just happens to be great.
And it's also like state, it manages the state.
So you can have the state of every execution within the workflow.
And you can trigger sub-workflows.
So you don't have to worry about managing a Kafka queue or a Redis or something like that on top of your code.
So it's all together.
And it's also, it's the first, it's one of the few workflow engines that the workflow is defined in pure code.
And the assumption that we have is that defining workflows in code makes it possible for coding generation LLMs to generate the workflow for you.
So instead of trying to teach that LLM how to generate a YAML or a JSON, you actually have to generate code with a well-structured framework.
And then you have a coding agent that can create workflows.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Discuss Pedro Steimbruch's potential role as a Senior Full Stack Engineer in the Client Operations and Applied AI team at growthx.ai.](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [growthx.ai is building an AI-enabled service as software platform, focusing on content generation and workflow automation for marketing agencies](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [The company is structured with a growth agency, platform team, and client operations engineering team](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [Pedro has extensive experience in full-stack development, particularly with Rails and React, and has worked on several successful startups](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [The role would involve working on the client operations team, creating custom workflows and contributing to the AI framework development](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Topics

### growthx.ai Overview

  - [Co-founded by Daniel Lopes and Marcel, combining engineering and marketing expertise](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [Raised funding in early 2023 and grew to about 70 people](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [Building an AI framework similar to LangChain, but in TypeScript and designed for code generation](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [Focused on creating efficient workflows for content generation and marketing tasks](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

### Company Structure and Teams

  - [Growth agency: Handles client relationships and content strategy](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [Platform team: Builds systems to support the agency](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [Client operations engineering team: Interfaces with the agency, creates custom workflows](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [Current engineering team size: 9 people, aiming to grow to 11-12 before pausing hiring](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

### Technology Stack and Development Process

  - [Main repos: Flow Studio, Atlas, and three core repos (platform, IDE, workflows)](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [Using Render for development environment and Temporal Cloud for workers](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [Flexible approach to pull requests, emphasizing senior developers' autonomy](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [Frequent deployments, with no strict mandatory review process](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

### Client Operations Team Role

  - [Create custom workflows for clients using the AI framework](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [Interface between agency needs and platform development](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [Contribute to improving the AI framework based on client demands](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [Blend of revenue generation and R\&D for the company](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

### Pedro's Background and Experience

  - [Nearly two decades of web development experience, starting with Rails in 2009](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=1165.0)
  - [Worked with consultancies and agencies before transitioning to early-stage startups](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=1254.0)
  - [Key roles in successful startups like Give.io and ProxyFix](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=1301.0)
  - [Co-founded Collateral XP, an appraisal management software company](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=1370.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Daniel to calibrate with Tucker and send a follow-up message to Pedro](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=4782.0)
  - [Potential start date after the AeroOps exit (in about two weeks)](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=4808.0)
  - [Possibility for Pedro to join the team gathering in San Francisco in early July](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=4821.0)
  - [Daniel to send additional information about the engineering culture to Pedro](https://fathom.video/share/6XantMAYMcwTuxrG3Z5exREJVcYsM-WT?tab=summary&timestamp=4881.0)
